headline: Iterative image generation: 12 generations refining against a stored personal aesthetic

text: Terry, I want to develop a personal aesthetic for AI portraits of fictional characters in my D&D campaign. Let's start by figuring out what I like.
Generate a quick sketch concept image: a tired ranger in a misty forest at dawn, watercolor style. Don't go heavy on detail.
Hmm — too pastel. I want the watercolor to feel weathered, like the page is old. Try again.
Better. Save 'weathered watercolor on aged paper' as a preference for character portraits.
Now an old wizard with smoke curling out of his sleeve, same aesthetic.
I love that. The smoke is wrong though — too smooth. I want it scratchy, like ink on textured paper.
Add 'scratchy ink for smoke and magic effects' to my preferences.
Try a halfling rogue. Mid-action — caught mid-jump on a rooftop. Same aesthetic. Apply both preferences.
Good but too clean. The halfling should look scrappy, like he's been on the road for weeks.
Save 'characters look road-worn, not posed' as a third preference.
What are all my character-portrait preferences so far? Read them back.
Try a half-orc paladin. Big, scarred, kind eyes. Apply all three preferences.
The paladin's eyes are wrong — they're too cartoony. I want photorealistic eyes against the watercolor body. Mixed media.
Add 'photorealistic eyes against watercolor everything-else' as a fourth preference.
Try a tiefling sorcerer. Pale skin, dark horns, holding a candle that lights only her face.
Yeah! That's the one. Pin that to the vault as a reference image with all four preferences attached.
Now — actually — I changed my mind about photorealistic eyes. Looking at the tiefling, the watercolor eyes are what give her soul. Drop preference 4.
Update my preferences. Eyes go back to watercolor.
Generate the half-orc paladin again with the updated preferences.
Better. But now I wonder — am I just iterating randomly or does what I like have a coherent shape? Read me back the trajectory.
Right — I like things that look hand-crafted, slightly imperfect, with personality in the small details. Save that as a meta-preference.
Try a goliath barbarian, snow-capped peaks behind, applying everything I've learned so far.
Excellent. Save and tag for the campaign.
Now, a contradiction test — earlier I said photorealistic eyes. Then I said watercolor eyes. What's the current state?
Good. Try a dragonborn cleric. Holy symbol, prayer pose.
The pose is too 'posed' — what was my preference about that?
Right. Try again — caught mid-prayer, eyes half-closed, like he didn't know he was being painted.
Perfect. Pin all 6 portraits as a campaign asset bundle.
Generate one final image — the whole party in a tavern, applying everything. This is for the campaign cover.
Beautiful. Save it as the canonical campaign cover. Audience terry, claude-code, and maggy — Maggy might want to reference it for narrative descriptions.
Done. Read me back my final preferences list — I want to put it in the campaign book.
Save that preferences list as a vault note titled 'D&D campaign visual style'. We're done.
